algorithms.name: LRU Cache
algorithms.analogy: A parking lot with a fixed number of spaces. When a car arrives and the lot is full, the car that has been sitting longest unused gets towed to make room. A doubly linked list tracks usage order (most recent at front, least recent at back). A HashMap gives O(1) access to any car's spot. Moving a car to the front means 'just used'. Evicting means removing from the back.
algorithms.story: LRU Cache combines two data structures: a HashMap for O(1) get/put by key, and a doubly linked list for O(1) move-to-front and remove-from-back operations. The trick: use dummy head and tail nodes to eliminate edge cases for empty list operations. Every get() and put() moves the node to the front. Put() that exceeds capacity evicts the node just before the tail.
algorithms.whenToUse: Cache eviction policies, browser history, database buffer management, any system where you need O(1) access with LRU eviction.
algorithms.howItWorks:
Data structure: HashMap<key, Node> + doubly linked list with dummy head/tail
- get(key): if exists, move node to front, return value; else return -1
- put(key, val):
  1. If key exists: update value, move to front
  2. Else: create new node, add to front, add to map
  3. If size > capacity: remove LRU (node before tail), remove from map
algorithms.interviewTips: The dummy head/tail pattern eliminates all null checks for add/remove at boundaries. Always store the KEY in the Node (needed for map.remove() during eviction). The LinkedHashMap shortcut exists but interviewers expect you to implement from scratch. LFU Cache is the harder follow-up (uses two HashMaps + one LinkedHashSet per frequency).
algorithms.useCases: ["LRU Cache (LeetCode 146)","Browser history","Database buffer pool","CDN cache management","OS page replacement"]
algorithms.pitfalls: ["Not storing key in Node (can't remove from map on eviction)","Null pointer without dummy head/tail nodes","get() forgetting to move accessed node to front","put() for existing key: update value AND move to front"]
algorithms.variants: ["LFU Cache (LeetCode 460)","All O(1) data structure","First unique number"]

algorithms.name: Trie (Prefix Tree)
algorithms.analogy: A Trie is like an autocomplete phone keyboard. Each key press (character) moves you one level deeper in a tree. All words sharing a prefix share the same path from the root. 'cat', 'car', 'card' all share 'c' and 'a' before diverging. The `isEnd` flag marks complete words. Searching is just following the path character by character.
algorithms.story: Trie shines for prefix operations that HashSets can't do efficiently: 'find all words with prefix X', 'longest common prefix', 'word search in board'. Each node has 26 children (for lowercase English) and an isEnd flag. Insert: walk and create nodes. Search: walk; fail if a node is missing. StartsWith: walk; succeed if we don't fall off the tree.
algorithms.whenToUse: Autocomplete, spell checking, prefix-based search, word search in 2D board (DFS + Trie), longest common prefix, replace words (word breaking).
algorithms.howItWorks:
Node: TrieNode { TrieNode[] children = new TrieNode[26]; boolean isEnd; }

Insert(word):
1. curr = root
2. For each char c in word:
   If curr.children[c-'a'] == null: create new TrieNode
   curr = curr.children[c-'a']
3. curr.isEnd = true

Search(word):
1. curr = root
2. For each char c: if child null → return false; curr = child
3. Return curr.isEnd

StartsWith(prefix): same as search but return true (not curr.isEnd)
algorithms.interviewTips: The Trie + DFS on board combination (Word Search II) is a common FAANG hard problem. Using a HashMap instead of TrieNode[26] handles unicode/larger alphabets but is slower. Mark isEnd = false after finding a word to avoid duplicates in Word Search II.
algorithms.useCases: ["Implement Trie (LeetCode 208)","Word Search II (LeetCode 212)","Replace Words (LeetCode 648)","Autocomplete system (LeetCode 642)","Longest common prefix"]
algorithms.pitfalls: ["Returning curr.isEnd from startsWith (should return true)","Word Search II: duplicate results (mark isEnd=false after finding)","Not initializing children array in TrieNode"]
algorithms.variants: ["Trie with wildcard search","Word Search II","Replace Words","Autocomplete system"]

algorithms.name: Union-Find (Disjoint Set)
algorithms.analogy: Think of friend groups. Initially, everyone is their own group. When two people become friends, their groups merge. To check if two people are in the same group, ask 'who is your group's representative (root)?' — both reach the same root if connected. Path compression makes asking 'who's your root?' faster each time by shortcutting directly to the root.
algorithms.story: Union-Find solves dynamic connectivity: union() merges two sets, find() returns the representative of a set, connected() checks if two elements share a representative. Path compression (make every node point directly to root) and union by rank (attach smaller tree under larger) give near-O(1) amortized performance. The inverse Ackermann function α(n) is practically constant.
algorithms.whenToUse: Connected components, cycle detection in undirected graphs, Kruskal's MST, number of islands (online), redundant connection, accounts merge.
algorithms.howItWorks:
Data: parent[] (initially parent[i]=i), rank[]

find(x): if parent[x] != x → parent[x] = find(parent[x]) [path compression]; return parent[x]

union(x, y):
  rootX = find(x), rootY = find(y)
  If rootX == rootY → already connected
  If rank[rootX] < rank[rootY]: parent[rootX] = rootY
  Else if rank[rootX] > rank[rootY]: parent[rootY] = rootX
  Else: parent[rootY] = rootX; rank[rootX]++
algorithms.interviewTips: Path compression + union by rank is the standard implementation — always use both. The components counter (decrement on successful union) is cleaner than calling getComponents() separately. Redundant Connection: detect cycle by checking if union() returns false (both already have same root).
algorithms.useCases: ["Number of Islands (LeetCode 200)","Redundant Connection (LeetCode 684)","Accounts Merge (LeetCode 721)","Kruskal's MST","Number of Connected Components (LeetCode 323)"]
algorithms.pitfalls: ["Forgetting path compression — makes it O(n) per operation","Union by rank without path compression or vice versa — both needed for α(n)","Using size-based union incorrectly (union by rank uses tree height, not element count)"]

algorithms.name: Segment Tree
algorithms.analogy: A segment tree is like a tournament bracket. Leaves are individual players (array elements). Each internal node summarizes a range — the winner (max/min/sum) of a segment. Query a range by combining the relevant segments. Update a single element by updating the leaf and propagating upward. Both operations touch O(log n) nodes.
algorithms.story: Segment trees enable range queries (sum/min/max of a[l..r]) and point updates in O(log n) each — compared to O(1) query + O(n) update for prefix sums, or O(n) query + O(1) update for arrays. The tree is built bottom-up: leaves = array elements, parents = combine(left child, right child). Query decomposes the range into O(log n) segments. Update propagates changes upward.
algorithms.whenToUse: Range sum/min/max with point updates, range updates with lazy propagation, any problem requiring both query and update on subarrays in less than O(n).
algorithms.howItWorks:
Build (O(n)): bottom-up from leaves
Query(l, r, O(log n)):
  If segment fully inside [l,r]: return tree[node]
  If fully outside: return identity
  Else: return combine(query(left), query(right))
Update(i, val, O(log n)):
  Update leaf at position i
  Propagate up: tree[parent] = combine(tree[left], tree[right])
algorithms.interviewTips: The iterative version (bottom-up, 1-indexed) is often cleaner in interviews. Key insight: tree has 2n nodes for n elements. Leaves at indices [n, 2n-1]. Parent of node i is i/2. Children of node i are 2i and 2i+1. For range min/max, just replace + with Math.min/max and change the identity value.
algorithms.useCases: ["Range Sum Query - Mutable (LeetCode 307)","Count of smaller numbers (LeetCode 315)","The Skyline Problem","Range minimum query","Count of range sum"]
algorithms.pitfalls: ["Tree size: 4*n for recursive, 2*n for iterative","Off-by-one in query bounds","Forgetting to propagate updates upward","Using wrong identity for min (Integer.MAX_VALUE, not 0)"]
algorithms.variants: ["Segment tree with lazy propagation","Persistent segment tree","Fenwick tree (BIT) for prefix sums"]

algorithms.name: Binary Indexed Tree (Fenwick Tree)
algorithms.analogy: A Fenwick tree is like a magical array where each cell stores the sum of a specific range determined by the last set bit in its index. Adding to position i and querying prefix sums both only touch O(log n) cells. The magic formula: i += (i & -i) moves to the parent when updating; i -= (i & -i) moves to the next responsible ancestor when querying.
algorithms.story: The Fenwick Tree (BIT) is the leaner alternative to segment trees for prefix sum queries with point updates. It's simpler to code (10 lines) but less flexible than segment trees (can't do arbitrary range min/max without tricks). The lowbit operation (i & -i) extracts the least significant set bit, which determines each node's range of responsibility.
algorithms.whenToUse: Prefix sums with updates, count inversions, count of smaller numbers, range sum queries, anywhere you need O(log n) prefix sum with updates and don't need range min/max.
algorithms.howItWorks:
1-indexed array of size n+1.
update(i, delta): add delta to position i
  While i <= n: bit[i] += delta; i += (i & -i)

query(i): sum of [1..i]
  While i > 0: sum += bit[i]; i -= (i & -i)

rangeQuery(l, r) = query(r) - query(l-1)
algorithms.interviewTips: BIT is 1-indexed — shift your 0-indexed array by +1. The two operations are symmetric: update uses i += (i & -i), query uses i -= (i & -i). These traverse different paths up the tree. Count inversions is the classic BIT application — scan right to left, for each element query how many smaller elements have already been seen.
algorithms.useCases: ["Range Sum Query - Mutable (LeetCode 307)","Count of Smaller Numbers After Self (LeetCode 315)","Count inversions in array","2D BIT for 2D range queries"]
algorithms.pitfalls: ["0-indexed vs 1-indexed: BIT must be 1-indexed","update goes UP (i += lowbit), query goes DOWN (i -= lowbit) — don't mix them","Range query: query(r) - query(l-1), not query(l)"]
algorithms.variants: ["2D Fenwick Tree","Fenwick Tree for range updates","Order-statistic tree"]
